issues.title: Jimmy Clay & Roy Kizer Golf Course Rezoning Initiation
issues.summary: Resolution initiating rezoning of approximately 445 acres of City-owned land (Jimmy Clay and Roy Kizer golf courses) at 5400 Jimmy Clay Drive. Sponsored by CMs Fuentes, Alter, Ellis, and Laine. This could be one of the largest single housing development opportunities in Austin if the land is eventually rezoned for residential/mixed-use.

public_comment.instructions: This was an initiation resolution — the actual rezoning process will have extensive public hearings. Monitor for future hearing dates.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: 445 acres of potential housing on city-owned land is a massive pro-development opportunity. Aligns directly with your YIMBY values. The initiation vote signals council intent; the actual rezoning fights will come later and will need organized pro-housing voices.

issues.title: Convention Center Redevelopment — Petition Rejected by Court
issues.summary: UPDATE (Feb 2026): Travis County District Judge Jessica Mangrum sided with Austin and upheld the City Clerk's rejection of the Austin United PAC petition to halt the $1.6 billion convention center redevelopment. The PAC had collected 25,000+ signatures but the Clerk's office certified it as ~500 signatures short. After a two-day trial in late January, the court ruled for the city. Mayor Watson called it 'a good win for Austinites.' The PAC has announced plans to organize a new petition targeting the November 2026 ballot. Construction on the new complex continues — expected to open by late 2028 with ~620,000 sq ft of rentable space (70% increase over the old center).

public_comment.instructions: Petition was rejected by court. PAC plans new petition for November ballot. Monitor for new signature-gathering efforts.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: The court ruling clears the way for the convention center project to proceed — a win for pro-development interests. However, Austin United PAC is regrouping for a November ballot push, so this could resurface. For now, this drops to background monitoring. The anti-development coalition (SOS Alliance backing) is worth tracking as it overlaps with NIMBY interests on housing.

issues.title: City Council District 1 — Open Seat Election
issues.summary:
CM Natasha Harper-Madison is term-limited. District 1 (East Austin, including 78702) will have an open seat for the first time in 8 years. Two declared candidates so far:

• Steven Brown, 41 — Born and raised in East Austin, works as a clinical specialist at Medtronic. Was co-chair of Save Austin Now alongside Matt Mackowiak (Travis County Republican Party chair), though he quit that role after Prop Q was defeated. His campaign positions him as anti-developer and constituent accountability focused. Residency concern: Brown listed a District 1 address on Single Shot Drive, but since 2019 he has owned a home with a homestead exemption on Ronee Leah Drive outside the district. Has raised ~$6,000 from two dozen donors.

• Alexandria Anderson, 39 — Retired world-class track and field sprinter, now runs Essential Fitness ATX. Active in Austin Neighborhoods Council and president of the MLK Neighborhood Association. Has owned her home on Cometa Street in District 1 since 2014. No fundraising reported yet.

Filing period: July 20 – August 17, 2026. More candidates expected.

public_comment.instructions: Monitor candidate announcements. Filing opens July 20. Evaluate housing/development positions carefully. Consider early engagement or donation to most-aligned candidate.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: This is YOUR district's council seat and it's open for the first time in 8 years. The winner will represent 78702 for the next 4 years on every housing and development vote. Steven Brown's Save Austin Now connection (co-chaired with Travis County GOP chair Matt Mackowiak) and anti-developer framing are red flags for YIMBY priorities. His residency outside the district is also worth watching. Anderson's neighborhood association roots suggest a different kind of candidacy but her housing positions are unknown. With filing not closing until August, the field will likely grow. This is the single most important local race for you in 2026.

issues.title: Commissioner Precinct 4 — Democratic Runoff: Morales vs Ledesma-Woody
issues.summary:
UPDATE (March 3 primary results): George Morales III (40.17%, 9,445 votes) and Susanna Ledesma-Woody (35.18%, 8,273 votes) advance to the May 26 runoff. Ofelia Maldonado Zapata (18.75%) and Gavino Fernandez Jr. (5.9%) were eliminated. Margaret Gómez is retiring after holding this seat since 1995. No Republican is running — the runoff winner takes the seat.

• George Morales III — Former elected Constable Precinct 4, current constable deputy. 20 years with Travis County government, including 16 years as an administrator on Commissioners Court focused on budgeting, housing, and community access.

• Susanna Ledesma-Woody — Project manager at AMD, 14-year Del Valle ISD school board trustee, founding member of Del Valle Community Coalition. Raised in Del Valle, has framed the race as 'policy and governance experience vs. policing experience.' This is her third run for this seat.

public_comment.instructions: Verify voter registration at votetexas.gov. Research both candidates' positions on development, transit, and infrastructure. Consider volunteering — small canvassing effort can shift outcomes in low-turnout runoffs.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: The primary narrowed the field to two candidates with very different backgrounds — a law enforcement veteran (Morales) vs. a policy/governance professional (Ledesma-Woody). Runoff turnout will be extremely low (8-12%), making your individual vote and any organizing effort outsized in impact. The commissioner directly influences county-level land use, transportation infrastructure, and budget decisions. Research both candidates' positions on development and transit — this is the highest impact-per-effort political action available to you in the next 60 days.

issues.title: Springdale Farm Zoning Follow-Up — District 3
issues.summary: Follow-up staff report on the Springdale Farm zoning case (C14-2026-0004) in District 3, near East Austin. A neighborhood postponement request was filed. This is an example of the neighborhood vs. development tension playing out at the parcel level. The Planning Commission heard this item on March 25 — monitor for outcome and next steps.

public_comment.instructions: Planning Commission meetings allow public comment. Check austintexas.gov for sign-up details.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Adjacent to your area. The neighborhood postponement tactic is a classic NIMBY delay strategy worth monitoring. Each individual case sets precedent for how similar cases in your neighborhood will be handled.

issues.title: Austin Housing Finance Corp — $5.5M Loan Authorization
issues.summary: Council authorized a $5.5 million AHFC loan for affordable housing. The council recesses its regular meeting to convene as the AHFC Board of Directors for these items.
issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Aligned with your housing values. Low contestedness means your engagement isn't needed here, but worth tracking as part of the city's overall housing investment picture.

issues.title: Raising Travis County — $5M Childcare Investment
issues.summary: Transfer of $5,046,004 from Raising Travis County fund to support interlocal agreement with City of Austin for childcare quality improvement and out-of-school time capacity, plus $80K for communications consulting.
issues.why_it_matters_to_you: Lower on your personal salience list but represents good governance — evidence-based investment in childcare infrastructure. The kind of pragmatic spending you'd support.

issues.title: Homelessness Strategic Plan Adopted — 650 New Shelter Beds Planned
issues.summary: Austin City Council approved the Homeless Strategy Office's (AHSO) first-ever strategic plan (2025-2027) on March 26. Key elements: 650 new shelter beds, 2 new housing navigation centers, $101M in proposed additional funding. Director David Gray noted ~6,500 people are currently living on the streets — 'one shelter bed for every five people sleeping unhoused.' Endeavors has taken over operation of downtown shelters (ARCH, Eighth Street) under an emergency contract through September 2026, in addition to the Marshalling Yard and Northbridge. A potential bond project could replace the Marshalling Yard with a new purpose-built shelter. Five focus areas: system leadership, impactful investments, crisis response, communications, and AHSO staff investment.

public_comment.instructions: The plan has been adopted. Budget hearings for FY2027 will determine how much of the $101M ask is funded. Monitor council budget sessions.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: This is the first comprehensive homelessness strategy Austin has ever adopted — a significant governance milestone. The $101M funding ask will compete with other priorities in the FY2027 budget. The plan's success or failure will directly affect neighborhood safety, public space quality, and the city's ability to pair housing density with livable streets. The dual-track framework (Housing First for some, treatment-first for others) aligns with your pragmatic approach. The Endeavors contract and potential Marshalling Yard bond project are concrete implementation steps to track.

issues.title: APD Staffing Audit — No Long-Term Recruitment Plan Found
issues.summary: A city audit found that APD 'does not have an effective strategy' to hire more officers despite years of staffing shortages. Key numbers: 340 vacancies out of 1,816 authorized positions (19% vacancy rate). APD's FY2026 budget is $540M (36.2% of the city's $1.5B general fund). A new $218M contract with the Austin Police Association provides a 28% pay raise over 5 years. Some positive signals: attrition has slowed (APD gained 3 officers net last year — first positive year in 5-6 years), and recruitment interest is up 166%. Chief Davis says APD could be fully staffed by end of 2027. The 154th cadet class began January 6, 2025. The 155th cadet class graduated December 12, 2025 with 38 new officers after a 32-week program. Chief Davis noted 102 candidates eligible for the next class, expressing optimism about scaling from classes of 30-50 to classes of 100.

public_comment.instructions: Monitor APD staffing reports at council. Budget hearings for FY2027 will address whether the $540M allocation is sufficient.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: APD staffing directly affects response times and public safety across Austin. The audit's finding that there's no recruitment plan despite 340 vacancies is an accountability failure worth tracking. The $218M contract and 28% pay raise are significant public investments — monitor whether they actually improve staffing. This connects to the broader 'safety is infrastructure' framework: adequate policing plus non-police crisis response (HOST teams) are both needed. The FY2027 budget will be the next decision point.

issues.title: HOME Initiative Delivering Results — Austin Rents Down 16%, Steepest Drop in US
issues.summary:
UPDATE (March 2026): A Pew Charitable Trusts report (March 18, 2026) confirms that Austin's housing construction surge — driven by the HOME Initiative and other pro-building policies — produced the steepest rent decline of any large U.S. city from 2021 to 2026. Key numbers:

• From 2015-2024, Austin added 120,000 units to its housing stock — a 30% increase, more than 3x the national rate (9%).
• Austin's median rent fell from $1,546 (Dec 2021) to $1,296 (Jan 2026) — a 16% decline.
• Austin's median rent is now 4% BELOW the U.S. median ($1,353) after being 15% above it in 2021.
• The combination of strong demand and proactive policy changes (HOME Phase 1 adopted Dec 2023, Phase 2 adopted May 2024) spurred the supply surge.

HOME Initiative implementation milestones: Phase 1 applications accepted Feb 2024, Phase 2 applications accepted Aug-Nov 2024. The initiative now permits up to 3 units on many single-family lots and reduced minimum lot sizes to 1,800 sq ft. Compatibility standards were also reformed — enforcement zones reduced from 540 feet to 75 feet, allowing more height flexibility near single-family homes.

This is concrete, measurable evidence that building more housing lowers costs. Austin is now the national proof point for YIMBY policy.

public_comment.instructions: The HOME Initiative is adopted and in implementation phase. Monitor for missing middle/mixed-use zoning reforms advancing through council in 2026-2027.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: This is the scoreboard. Austin's 16% rent decline — the steepest in any large US city — is direct evidence that the YIMBY policy agenda works. Building more housing lowers costs. This data is your most powerful argument in every future rezoning fight, council race, and public hearing. The Pew report gives this national credibility. Share it. Cite it. Use it to counter every 'building doesn't help' argument you'll hear in the golf course rezoning and council election debates.

issues.title: Missing Middle & Mixed-Use Zoning Expansion — Advancing Through Council
issues.summary:
UPDATE (March 26, 2026): Austin City Council advanced the expansion of missing middle residential and mixed-use zoning, building on the HOME Initiative. The proposal creates new zoning categories for townhomes, cottage courts, single-stair buildings, and other 'missing middle' housing types.

A January 2026 city zoning study recommended allowing 4-6 units on lots of at least 5,750 square feet. The new zones would also allow neighborhood-scale mixed-use (retail on ground floor, housing above) in appropriate locations.

The initiative builds on HOME Phase 1 & 2 and represents the next phase of Austin's zoning reform agenda. Once finalized and approved (expected 2027), further community discussions will determine where the new allowances apply.

This is the natural next step after HOME: having allowed more units on single-family lots, the city is now creating purpose-built zoning categories for the kind of gentle density that makes neighborhoods walkable and transit-supportive.

public_comment.instructions: Community engagement ongoing through 2026-2027. Monitor austintexas.gov/development-services/home-amendments for public hearing schedules.

issues.why_it_matters_to_you: This is HOME Initiative Phase 3 in everything but name. The missing middle zoning categories create purpose-built pathways for the gentle density that makes neighborhoods walkable — townhomes, cottage courts, small mixed-use buildings. This is especially relevant for 78702 where transit-oriented development along the future light rail corridor needs exactly this kind of zoning. Support this through public comment when hearings are scheduled.
